era_context: The late Roman Empire under Theodosius I, who ruled the Eastern Roman Empire (379–395 CE) and briefly reunified the empire (394–395 CE). Christianity was rapidly becoming the dominant religion following Constantine's legalization in 313 CE; Theodosius would make it the state religion in 380 CE itself. The Western Roman Empire was fragmenting under pressure from Germanic migrations and internal instability. The Eastern Empire faced threats from the Goths, Persians, and internal religious schism.

events.title: Edict of Thessalonica (Cunctos Populos)
events.description: Theodosius I and co-emperors Gratian and Valentinian II issued the Edict of Thessalonica on 27 February 380 CE, declaring Nicene Christianity (Trinitarian orthodoxy) the official state religion of the Roman Empire. The edict condemned Arian, Apollinarian, and other heterodox Christian sects as heretical and subject to legal penalties. This marked the first time a Roman emperor used state power to enforce religious orthodoxy and effectively ended the period of Christian pluralism within the empire. The edict had profound consequences for the development of Christian theology, church-state relations, and the persecution of religious minorities.

sources.name: Codex Theodosianus (Theodosius II's law code, compiled 438 CE, preserving the text of the edict)
Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine (written early 4th century, discusses Theodosius's religious policies)
Socrates Scholasticus, Ecclesiastical History (written early 5th century)

events.certainty_note: The edict is preserved in the Codex Theodosianus and is one of the most securely dated events of this period. The date of 27 February 380 CE is well-attested.
cross_references: Follows from Constantine's legalization of Christianity (313 CE)
Led to First Council of Constantinople (381 CE)
Part of broader Christianization of the Roman Empire

events.title: Battle of the Frigidus River (preliminary campaigns)
events.description: Theodosius I conducted military campaigns against Gothic and other barbarian incursions along the Danube frontier in 380 CE. These campaigns were part of his broader strategy to stabilize the Eastern Empire's borders following the Gothic victory at Adrianople in 378 CE. While the major Battle of the Frigidus River would occur in 394 CE, the 380 CE campaigns represent the beginning of Theodosius's military consolidation and his eventual settlement with the Goths through the foederati system (treaty-based alliance).
key_figures: Alaric (Gothic leader, though his prominence came later)

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Res Gestae (written late 4th century, covers military campaigns)
Zosimus, New History (written early 6th century, Byzantine historian)

events.certainty_note: Theodosius's military activities in 380 CE are attested in later sources, but specific battle details are sparse. The broader campaign context is secure; exact chronology of individual engagements is less certain.
cross_references: Follows from Battle of Adrianople (378 CE)
Led to foederati treaties with Goths (382 CE)

events.title: Consolidation of Theodosius I's authority in the Eastern Empire
events.description: In 380 CE, Theodosius I had been ruling the Eastern Roman Empire for approximately one year (since 379 CE). He worked to consolidate his authority, suppress usurpers, and stabilize the empire after the catastrophic loss at Adrianople. His policies combined military reorganization, religious orthodoxy enforcement (via the Edict of Thessalonica), and diplomatic settlement with barbarian groups. This year marks a turning point in his consolidation of power, which would culminate in his brief reunification of the entire Roman Empire in 394–395 CE.
events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Theodosius's consolidation are well-attested; specific events within 380 CE are less detailed in surviving sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Theodosius I reign (379–395 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The Edict of Thessalonica is sometimes portrayed as an immediate, total suppression of non-Nicene Christianity, but modern scholarship (e.g., Ramsay MacMullen) notes that enforcement was gradual and uneven. Arian and other heterodox communities persisted for centuries in various regions, particularly among Germanic peoples and in the Eastern Empire's periphery. The edict's immediate practical impact may have been overstated in earlier historiography.
historiographic_note: The year 380 CE is exceptionally well-documented for the Roman Empire, particularly regarding the Edict of Thessalonica, which is preserved in legal compilations. However, sources are predominantly written from the perspective of the imperial court and the Nicene Christian establishment. The experiences of non-Nicene Christians, pagans, and barbarian groups are underrepresented. Sources on military campaigns and frontier conditions are less detailed than for political and religious events.

graph_edges.note: The Edict of Thessalonica prompted the convocation of the First Council of Constantinople in 381 CE to further define Nicene orthodoxy.
Military campaigns of 380 CE led to the foederati treaty with the Goths in 382 CE.
The crisis following Adrianople (378 CE) created conditions for Theodosius's religious consolidation.
